chunks.text: We are now in public. These petitioners are these funders, so we want them to see about our talk. OK, that's fine. All right, do we have Ms Kane? Can you hear me? Yes, we can. Can you hear me now? Yes. Fantastic. All right, thank you now. Now, just before you start, just we'll introduce ourselves.

I'm Greg O'Connor, I'm the chair of the committee. On my left is... Kia ora, Francisco Hernandez, Green. Kia ora. Ryan Hamilton, National Party. Hi, Robin. I'm Paolo Garcia, Newland MP. All right. Thank you, Robin. We've got your submission, and we're looking forward to hearing.

We've got you down for 10 minutes. You can use that how you like, but it'll be nice to leave some time at the end for questions. Go ahead. OK, I'll just introduce myself. So, Tena koutou, Tena koutou, Tena koutou katoa, kamehiki koutou katoa. Kor Kaitahu mea, kati mamoi, oku iwi, kor Joss Tokuhapu, kor Robin Kane Tokuikua.

Now, I just want to bring to your attention. Well, first of all, I just want to say everything that I do, what that I say, is done under the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act, Freedom of Expression, and the Protected Disclosures Act. And that is because I have found I am able to get into the justice system that we actually have to buy justice in New Zealand, even though our constitutional laws, being the Magna Carta 1297, says no man shall buy justice.

And the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act says that justice is a human right, and human rights are inherent. Something You Buy As What The United Nations Says. So I have no way of getting into the justice system to get justice for the laws that have been broken against me. And because I cannot get access to justice which is a fundamental part of the rule of law, that means that Parliament and Government does not have rule of law.

I'm actually under the laws of wealthy corporations and businesses aa yungexculant,Нai te iwi ka unidiais. Ngai New Zealandais mai ranaifnit yw pacoolan, gan dillastu syni admitted aheadhellain. Pa athwai bet Speedwark po so praka'y maa metulau maipika. I pan, lu, d hyung apau pu其 maipika paНosat wanio pa kuk strengths un στην modu melaie boards maive in i breل athiere i maiai anaitai buzure maillai i atribusiazio. Ia poundi te rei atibaisyt o pewa tawa?

Te rei atibai maiai anaitai. Pa o i atibai. Ngai te rei atibai te hustle, pamanapa tadaa avieite me-t ت Library te kairi atibiai te atibiaa. Ia maiai ka naita i tetea sisi. Pa mauna re reviite kogu nila te tetea sisi pa tataa. Pa mauna tadaa maiai te atibiai in the land.

kaito, pesama. Ka maori, naitahu apopara ngak i siari wa i maqti maqti. Ako i ki pa pasiando Westfield, maikai di ma blartu i sayari maia. Di w Char, disanatara pa i maqti maqti maia. Pa i kaito i ka maqti maia. Pa i pa i i maqti maia, i maqui nakaame, i maqti maia.

Til maqti maia i kaito i ka maqti, i maqti maia. So, the justice system is actually violating those two constitutional laws and it means we don't have human rights in New Zealand if we can't get into the justice system. And we have agreed with the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and the Civil and Political Agreement that we signed back in the 1970s to uphold human rights in this country, but I've clearly found that we are not.

There's no way of getting the human right to justice. There's no way of getting other human rights because we cannot get into the justice system and the laws can't be upheld for a whole lot of us that can't afford to pay for justice. So, your particular blockage or case, did it relate to a Ngai Tahu claim?

Would that be fair? No, it didn't. It's just I'm just simply telling you that you have to pay for justice in this country and unless you pay for it, you cannot get the system, you cannot get justice and yet justice is a human right. So, we're all entitled to it. So, why are we paying for it? Why are we being made to pay to get into the justice system?

That is a Crown organisation and if they're asking for money, then they're asking for assets and lands that they're measuring us against, the assets and lands of Ngai Tahu people and other Māori and yet we have ahead our settlements and that looks like it wasn't reconciled because we are still losing our land and our assets to the Crown through the justice system and we still can't enter. There's absolutely no way of entering without being able to pay.
to pay, so we do not have the human right to justice in New Zealand. All right, members, any questions?

All right, Mr Hernandez. Thank you, chair. Thank you so much for your suggestion. I think we all agree that access to justice should be as barrier free as possible, but would you put in place any limits or criteria to access this? For example, would you cap it at a certain amount each year or would you only do it in the event of, like, there's like, when you're the defendant party or are you proposing unlimited legal aid for everything, for everyone, for any reason?

Well, I'm not talking about legal aid because legal aid is alone. It's still paying. So at the end of the day. A free version of that. Yeah, what I'm saying is that justice is supposed to be free. So in my submission, I have said, if you've got 96,000 pages of legislation which you have got more than that many, how can anybody possibly follow the law in this country?

We need all the things that I pointed out that we need in this and I want an inquiry into this because it doesn't just affect me. This will affect hundreds of thousands of people in New Zealand. But I have just stuck it out for six years trying every which way to get in and recording everything I have done so that I have all my gear ready for an inquiry.

And I'm sure a lot more people will come and join in the inquiry and give you a lot more information so you actually can do something about it. So what I have put, if you read my submission, is we need a constitution. So all those constitutional laws are clear. We also need it so we have civics taught in our school so everybody understands and everything they do, whether they are following the law or whether they are not following the law.

We need a presidential style... Minister. So that all decisions made in Parliament can be done with consensus, 90% consensus so we don't have the tail-weaving of the dog. And we also need to do more problem-solving with members of the public to solve problems and involve them in more things, which will give us more justice and more human rights. It will unearth these human rights issues that are lying below the surface and not coming out in the justice system to be dealt with or in politics for that matter. And yeah, there's so much can be done so I've

written down a whole lot of those recommendations and I think the people in New Zealand need to add their ideas. So I would want an inquiry, which is the best way to deal with the situation. All right, look, there's no other question from any other members. I want to thank you for taking the time this afternoon to make and to send in your submission. What will happen now as the committee will consider your submission. And we've also had some a report from the Ministry of Justice as well, which will sit alongside that.
So thank you very much for your time. Very much appreciated. Thank you. Thank you. Right, bye.
